data.title: Lightspeed Commerce: Supplier Integration Might Not Be The Best POS Strategy
data.description:
In the quantitative department and according to my analysis, Lightspeed looks like a good pick. But if we look closely at LSPD strategy we can infer that it is not working as expected. In addition, competition in the POS business is high because it encompasses Fintechs and Banks. Because the quantitative and the qualitative fields don’t define Lightspeed as a buy, I’m rating it as a hold.

Background

Lightspeed (LSPD) is a company focused on deploying POS systems and payment processing in its merchants. Initially Lightspeed focused on developing the Point-of-Sale software, improving the management of inventory, sales, orders and analytics, in retail, which is its biggest revenue segment, but also in hospitality. Then Lightspeed expanded to payment processing and more recently is enforcing merchants using the POS software to use its payment processing system. Merchants that don’t migrate to LSPD’s payments are charged more fees, while the ones that join LSPD’s payments get a negotiated take rate that looks to match or beat the previous third-party take rate.

Lightspeed has a strong presence in specific retail businesses, such as golf courses, developing vertically integrated solutions that enhance its POS system and protect its moat around those specific retailers.

To improve vertical integration in its retailers, in 2021 LSPD acquired NuOrder for 425 million. Considering that LSPD market cap sits at 1.3 billion dollars, it was a massive acquisition and strategic move. NuOrder works with more than 500 thousand retailers and 3000 brands, allowing retailers to order and sell, in a B2B format, goods from the international brands.

Strategy

Improve retailers’ vertical integration, by acquiring NuOrder and integrating other suppliers. By integrating and buying supply-driven platforms, Lightspeed is improving the ordering capabilities, inventory management and automating analytics processes. If the retailer uses NuOrder to supply its business it will be able to understand how much inventory is available in NuOrder, making the retailer buy more inventory if the good is being oversold in store and there is a lot of competition for it in NuOrder. The POS system and the supplier are directly connected, so the retailer can measure quickly inventory in store and in transit, leading to the automation of back-office processes and better visibility of the overall inventory. In addition, the orders are integrated in the POS system. As a result, retailers have more visibility of when the order is arriving at the store.

In the latest earnings call the company’s management said this:

Second, as you know, we've connected effectively our new order wholesale platform with our point of sale. And so at the point of sale level now, you can actually see the inventory of your suppliers, which is really a unique proposition that no one is able to offer and we're really pleased with the progress there. We've seen over 2,000 POS customers connecting to the supplier network and that number is growing significantly.

NuOrder acquisition brought a large number of retailers to Lightspeed’s ecosystem, facilitating cross-selling opportunities for LSPD’s POS and payment processing, and for NuOrder supply. Despite the heavy price tag, NuOrder acquisition is bringing differentiation to the POS software capabilities and opening a large market for LSPD.

Financials

I would like to compare Lightspeed to Shift4 (FOUR) because both follow different strategies and work in similar segments of the market. Shift4 executes more horizontal acquisitions, expanding internationally by buying new companies, but also does, with less intensity, vertical acquisitions, for example, recently acquiring Givex that specializes in loyalty and gift cards solutions. Lightspeed engages more in vertical integration, acquiring NuOrder for a large sum, but also enters in horizontal acquisitions, such as Vend, an Asia-Pacific retailer POS provider.

Lightspeed has much higher gross margins because it generates 1/3 of the revenue via POS SaaS subscriptions, while Shift4 generates 1/10 of the revenue with SaaS subscriptions. SaaS subscriptions revenue has higher gross margins than payment processing revenue. On the other hand, Shift4 has increased very substantially its gross margins YoY while Lightspeed decreased them slightly.

Shift4 has a much higher Gross Profit/Employed Capital, however, Lightspeed has been increasing this value significantly in the last year. Despite Shift4 being known for a large number of acquisitions and acquisition fueled growth, it has much less Goodwill as a percentage of total assets than Lightspeed.

If we look at the growth estimates, we can see that Shift4 is projected to grow revenue at a faster pace than Lightspeed. Yet, the latter has been restructuring to balance revenue growth with opex. As a result, it will increase the non-GAAP earnings growth.

Shift4 is much more valued than Lightspeed using a Price/Gross Profit multiple. The revenue is growing faster, its gross profit generation is more efficient than Lightspeed, while boasting very high EBITDA margins.

Opportunities

LSPD keeps innovating and expanding its POS capabilities. One area where I believe they can make a difference while increasing market acceptance is by deploying advanced analytics in the merchant’s retailers. The company recently introduced the Retail Insights tool. It enables the retailer to forecast sales, manage inventory and orders by using ML and heuristics. The management of the store is much more automated and streamlined, being a perfect solution for mid-sized retailers that work with multiple stores .

In the latest earnings call, the company’s management said this:

On the product and technology front, I'll highlight how recently introduced software offerings are already contributing to our software revenue growth, as well as some of the newer releases this quarter. The retail insights module, which we launched last quarter, is already contributing to our software growth and is enabling merchants to improve their gross margins by a 16% reduction in out-of-stock days for popular items.

Lightspeed also developed a tool that allows restaurants to benchmark to the overall industry (serviced by Lightspeed), providing a competing solution to the more established Toast (TOST). I would like to see LSPD develop a similar solution for each of its retail segments, a high-value proposition for the store owners.

Risks

LSPD tends to invest large sums in premium acquisitions that might not translate into good capital allocation.

The company is not profitable yet while its growth is lower than Shift4’s, if growth contracts, we could see a major downgrade in the company's multiples.

Valuation

To understand what growth the market is pricing, I like to do a reverse discounted cash flow analysis. To model the discount rate, I use 10%. To be conservative, I assumed a starting FCF margin of 1%, that expands to 8% (slightly lower than Shift4’s current TTM 12% FCF margin). To generate the terminal multiple, I’m using a terminal growth rate of 4.7%. The terminal growth rate is obtained summing a real GDP growth rate of 2.5% and an inflation rate (since 2000) of 2.2%, resulting in the number 4.7%. I also take into consideration the share count to calculate the FCF per share. It expands by 2.5% every year. With the inputs, the growth is balanced to obtain the current share price.

According to the model inputs, the market is pricing a growth that starts at 9.8% in 2025 and reduces to 5% every year, reaching 6.1% in 2034. The company looks cheap for the growth that analysts are predicting.

Conclusion

LSPD is executing its differentiation strategy by integrating vertically with its merchants’ suppliers. For now, that strategy as translated into low performance, with only 2000 merchants being integrated with their suppliers' data, from a pool of more than 150 000 merchants. Lightspeed's costly acquisition of NuOrder may not have delivered the impact the company had anticipated. The company looks cheap from a DCF analysis perspective. Despite this, bad capital allocation poses risks for future share returns.

I like that Lightspeed is trying to differentiate by being the most vertically integrated POS supplier, but maybe, it is not the best strategy for a POS provider. Since my strategy is to be selective while buying and holding for the long term, LSPD doesn’t fit my style due to questionable strategy and capital allocation. I rate LSPD a hold.

data.title: Intel: Don't Bet On A Quick Turnaround
data.description:
Intel’s (NASDAQ:INTC) new CEO, Lip-Bu Tan, recently gave a keynote presentation where he laid out his vision for the company. This included a wide range of topics. Some of the most positive aspects of the speech were a more innovative culture and having a startup mindset. Earlier, Tan had mentioned that he would need to address the slow-moving and bloated middle management. Deep job cuts could help change the culture and allow the CEO to invest in areas that require greater priority. However, Tan also reiterated the approach of his predecessor in building a strong foundry business. I believe the foundry business would require a very long time to deliver results that Intel does not have. The focus of the foundry business will also take away crucial resources that are needed to compete in AI chips. In the previous article, I upgraded Intel stock to Hold. Since then, the stock has seen a 40% jump and has settled back to the low $20s range.

Intel has entered the risk production for its latest 18A chips and is planning on commercial production in the second half of this year. There have been 27 downward EPS revisions in the last 90 days, compared to only one upgrade. This shows that Wall Street is still not convinced by the approach of Intel and will take a few more quarters to see any positive improvement. The EPS estimate for the fiscal year ending Dec 2026 is $1.14, which gives the stock a forward PE of 19.3. This is quite high when we look at other semiconductor stocks that have a better growth trajectory.

Pros and cons of the recent keynote event

The new CEO laid out his vision for Intel in a recent keynote event. There were many positive factors mentioned during this speech. One of the best was a desire to change the culture of Intel. Instead of focusing on some products, the new CEO spent a lot of time discussing the new culture that is required at Intel. The word “culture” was mentioned more than 20 times in the speech, which shows the strong inclination of the new CEO to change things from the bottom up. He also mentioned having a new startup mindset in the company, which can drive faster innovation. Previously, Tan has already mentioned that Intel has a very bloated middle management. Hence, it is very likely that we could see deep job cuts in Intel over the next few quarters. This would allow the management to invest resources in more important areas.

Figure: Recent EPS revisions of Intel. Source: Seeking Alpha

Over the last 90 days, Intel has seen 27 downward revisions and only 1 upward revision. This clearly shows the poor sentiment towards the stock on Wall Street. It will take a number of quarters to change this trajectory, and we should not expect any quick results from the new CEO.

A big negative within the keynote speech was a continuation of the foundry plan laid out by the previous CEO. I believe it would be very difficult for Intel to compete with TSMC (TSM) and build a strong market share in this segment. The foundry business requires massive investments and a long time to deliver results. Intel does not have this much time, and poor sentiment on Wall Street could also limit the ability of the company to absorb any headwinds in this segment. Intel’s CEO mentioned that he is “equally committed to building a strong foundry.”

Intel has already delayed the Ohio chipmaking site to the next decade. Initially, this fab was supposed to start in 2025. This later got pushed to 2027, and now it has been delayed to 2030. It is not clear how the new CEO intends to deal with these delays. Another big disadvantage of focusing on the foundry business is that it takes limited resources away from the AI segment. Intel has fallen behind Nvidia (NVDA) and AMD (AMD) in building an attractive AI chip business. Intel would need to make a lot of investment to build a strong AI chip business. Hence, any diversion of resources in foundry business can be a big negative for the overall potential of the company.

Big job cuts and R&D expense

Intel’s new CEO has already mentioned that the company has a bloated middle management that moves very slowly. This hurts the ability of the company to deliver new products aggressively. Intel has already announced massive job cuts of 15% of its workforce in August 2024. The focus of the new CEO on changing the culture of Intel and removing excess staff could mean we see another big job cut announcement from Intel. I believe we could hear a job cut announcement of at least 20% to 30% in the near term. This will improve the cash flow for the company and also lift the EPS over the next few quarters.

Figure: Intel’s EPS estimate in the next few fiscal years. Source: Seeking Alpha

For the fiscal year ending Dec 2026, Intel is expected to deliver EPS of $1.14 which gives the stock a forward PE of 19.30. This PE is still quite high when we compare it with other chipmakers. Another negative is the massive gap between the lowest and highest forward EPS estimates for Intel. The lowest EPS estimate for the fiscal year ending Dec 2026 is $0.60, while the highest is $2.00. Hence, the highest forward EPS estimate is more than thrice the lowest EPS estimate. This shows the massive uncertainty in the ability of Intel to deliver good EPS in the next few quarters.

Intel’s R&D expense is significantly higher than that of Nvidia, AMD and TSM. In the trailing twelve months, Intel’s R&D expense was $16.5 billion, while Nvidia was $12.9 billion, AMD was $6.5 billion, and TSM was $6.4 billion. This shows that despite massive research expenses, Intel has not been able to deliver attractive products. Massive job cuts at Intel could change this issue by bringing in new talent that delivers better results.

Maintain a “Hold” rating

In the previous article, I upgraded Intel’s stock to a Hold rating from a Sell rating. The main reason behind this upgrade was a change in future plans by the new management. Since the upgrade, Intel stock went up by 40% to $27 and has again settled to its previous range of $20 to $22. Despite a massive culture change at Intel, it will take some time to see actual results. As mentioned above, we will also need to see how the new management deals with the foundry business. A big investment in foundry could further delay the turnaround for the company. Job cuts are a positive factor, but we need to see improvement in the product lineup to build strong bullish momentum. It is unlikely that Wall Street is going to change its sentiment towards Intel only on the announcements of Intel.

Even for two fiscal years ahead, Intel’s forward PE multiple of 13.2 is not significantly cheaper than other chip stocks. AMD is trading at $103 with an EPS estimate of $7.5 for the two fiscal years ahead. This gives the stock a forward PE of only 13.7, despite having a better AI product line than Intel. Even TSM is trading at a forward PE of less than 15. All these companies have better growth potential, while Intel is trying to make a strong turnaround. We could see sideways momentum in Intel stock for a few more quarters before any concrete results are reported by the company.

Investor Takeaway

In the recent keynote event, Intel’s new CEO Lip-Bu Tan has laid out an aggressive vision for the company. He mentioned changing the culture of the company and will likely make deep job cuts to reduce the massive middle management. However, he also mentioned that Intel will continue to focus on the foundry business, which can take a long time to deliver results. Intel has already delayed the launch of its Ohio plant till 2030.

The R&D expense of Intel is still larger than Nvidia, AMD, and TSM. If the new management makes big changes in talent acquisition, Intel has the resources to build a strong product lineup. This will take a few more quarters, which is why a Hold rating is given to the stock. Intel stock does not have a big discount in PE when compared to other competitors. This further reduces the upside for the stock from the current price point.

data.title: Friday: Tariffs, U.S. Jobs, Powell, And Melting Equities
data.description:
Overview

There appear to be two-forces at work that have helped the US dollar recover. First, as the market continues to debate whether the reciprocal tariffs are a negotiating ploy and in this tug-of-war of sorts, President Trump's declaration that he is open to "phenomenal offers" plays into that view. Still, the fact that Israel got rid of all of its tariffs on the US and still was hit with a 17% levy is notable. Second, there may be some position-adjustment ahead of the US jobs report. So far, the weakness in the US labor market appears in the soft, survey data, not the hard data, like weekly initial jobless claims. The rise in continuing claims is consistent with slowing in employment. A few hours after the jobs report, Fed Chair Powell will speak about the economy. This could be another source of volatility. However, we suspect he will be reluctant to signal much concern about the volatility of the stock market, i.e., no resurrection of the simplistic--stock market falls sharply, and Fed will cut" so-called Fed put.

The US dollar is firmer against all the G10 currencies but the Swiss franc. German factory orders disappointed, and BOJ Governor Ueda recognized the unsettled global environments. Emerging market currencies are mixed, with Asia-Pacific outperforming Central Europe. The PBOC set the dollar's reference rate at its highest for the year. Few equity markets have escaped the selling pressure and US index futures are off 0.5%-1.0%, which warns of the gap lower opening. Bonds are still the safe haven. European 10-year yields are off 6-10 bps, and the peripheral premiums are widening. The 10-year US Treasury yield (US10Y) is off eight basis points to about 3.95%. Gold, ironically, is trading more like a risk asset and is off 0.75% today, but above yesterday's low (~$3054). May WTI has extended yesterday's dramatic sell-off, spurred by OPEC+ decision to boost sales around three-times more than expected and broader growth concerns. The contract reached about $64.25, its lowest since last September.

USD: The dollar fell sharply and broadly yesterday. The Dollar Index dropped by almost 1.7%, its largest drop since November 2022. It fell from a high Wednesday near 104.30 to a low a near 101.25 but managed to settle above slightly102.00. The sell-off was so sharp that the Dollar Index spent most of the European and North American session below three standard deviations from the 20-day moving average (~112.20). The Bollinger Bands are set two standard deviations away from the 20-day moving average. Three is an extreme event, if the returns are not normally distributed. After sliding back to nearly101.50, DXY recovered to the first area of technical resistance may be in the 102.40-50 area, near where the lower Bollinger Band is found. Above there, the 102.80-103.00 may offer stronger resistance. It is hard to reconcile claims that America has been taken advantage of when its outperformance, whether since the Global Financial Crisis or Covid fanned discussions framed around "US exceptionalism." The US GDP has never been higher, and US household net worth has never been greater. Rather than a bold new initiative for growth, the US is pursuing a more intensive extractive strategy, similar to when ancient Rome wanted to fill up its coffers and sent out "tax farmers" to the provinces. While investors continue to digest the United States' latest salvo of tariffs, attention turns to the US jobs data. While surveys, like the ISM manufacturing and service surveys, find a deterioration in the labor market, the weekly jobs claims, including for federal government employees, show little evidence. The ADP private sector jobs estimate was stronger than expected, at 155k. There is little reason to expect it to show up in today's nonfarm payrolls. In the six months through February, nonfarm payrolls rose by 191k. In the six months through February 2024, nonfarm payrolls rose by 183k. The median forecast in Bloomberg's survey is for a 140k increase in March (151k in February), of which 135k (140k in February) came from the private sector. The household survey generates the unemployment rate, and a tick up (from 4.0%) should not be surprising. There is a deep conviction that the labor market will deteriorate this year, and we suspect it will be showing up in the data by the end of the quarter. Ahead of today's employment report and Fed Chair Powell's speech about four hours later, the futures market is discounting about slightly more than a 90% chance of a Fed cut in June, up from around 2/3 change at the start of last week.

Euro: The euro spiked to nearly $1.1145 in early North American trading yesterday but settled near North American session lows below $1.1030. It poked above $1.1100 briefly but met sellers. A combination of disappointing German factory orders and position adjustments ahead of the US jobs report saw the euro push below $1.10, triggering stop-loss selling that extended initially toward $1.0965. The US two-year premium over Germany narrowed by eight basis points over the past two days to its lowest in around four weeks. EMU's aggregate calendar is quiet, but the largest four members reported real sector data today. Germany's February factory orders were flat but had been expected to rise after the 7.0% drop in January that was revised to -5.5%. To be sure, it is a volatile series, like US durable goods orders. They averaged -0.4% in 2024, but in a range of -10.9% to +6.2%. France reported a 0.7% increase in February industrial output, its first gain since last September, led by a 1.4% increase in manufacturing production. Spain reported a 0.7% gain in February industrial production, better than expected, but in the previous three months, it fell by a nearly a cumulative 2.0%. Italy, which may be hit among the hardest by US tariffs, reported a 0.1% increase in February retail sales, leaving them down by about 0.25% in the first two months of the year. Meanwhile, money fleeing US equities does not appear to be going into European equities. The Stoxx 600 (STOXX) index is off around 9.5% from the record high seen in early March.

CNY: The dollar reached a two-month high against the offshore yuan yesterday near CNH7.35 in Asia, before being pushed back to CNH7.2750 by late in the European session, as the greenback came under broad and sharp pressure. It fell slightly below CNH7.24 today, a new low for the week. On the other hand, the PBOC fixed the dollar higher (CNY7.1889 vs. CNH7.1793 yesterday), and matches the highest reference rate of the year. China may report its March reserve figures over the weekend. Lending figures are also due next week, but the data highlight is the March CPI and PPI next Thursday. While deflation in producer prices continues (-2.3% expected vs. -2.2%), the deflation in consumer prices (-0.7%) appears to have been a statistical quirk. It was the first negative reading since January 2024. It is seen flat in February, year-over-year, compared with 0.7% in February 2024. Yesterday, Fitch cut China's sovereign rating to A from A+, the lowest of the top 3 rating agencies, but matches DBRS assessment. Fitch cited concerns about weaker public finances (debt fueled spending) and the impact of US tariffs. The rating agency said the decision was made before yesterday's reciprocal tariffs were announced.

JPY: The US 10-year yield briefly traded below 4% for the first time since the middle of last October, facilitated by plunging stock market. The dollar was crushed from a high near JPY149.30 to JPY145.20. It settled nearly three standard deviations below its 20-day moving average, which was near JPY145.40. It held above JPY145.30 today and recovered to JPY146.55 to enter its Bollinger Band. The volatility is such that a move into the JPY147.00-20 on a favorable reaction to the US jobs data cannot be ruled out. The yen and Swiss franc both appreciated by more than 2% yesterday. What they both have in common is low interest rates and the traditional appeal as funding currencies, when they are borrowed and sold for a higher yielding instrument or another asset, from emerging market bonds to equities. As those assets fall, which invariably they will, the asset is sold and the funding currency bought back. Also, among some participants there is a belief that their net international investment position is the first line of defense and when risk is off, some of those assets can be repatriated. Both give a sense of "safe haven." Japan reported household spending fell by 0.8% year-over-year in February. In February 2024, it fell 0.5% year-over-year. In GDP terms, consumption 0.1% in Q4 and appears to have remained subdued this quarter. This is an inflation adjusted report. Low unemployment, rising nominal wages, and government subsidies for energy have proven insufficient to boost consumption.

GBP: Sterling raced up to almost $1.3210 yesterday. It stalled and some profit-taking appeared to kick-in. Sterling eased slightly through $1.3080 in late turnover. It reached almost $1.3115 today before being sold to around $1.2960. Despite sterling being slapped with a 10% tariff and the EU 20%, the euro has surged against sterling and approached the year's high today near GBP0.8470 (from ~GBP0.8320 yesterday). After spending most of the last 10 months of 2024 above the 50 boom/bust level, UK's construction PMI has spent Q1 25 contracting. It stood at 46.3 in March (44.6 in February).

CAD: The US dollar fell by a little more than 1% against the Canadian dollar to approach CAD1.4025, its lowest since early last December. The 200-day moving average is near CAD1.40. The push through CAD1.41 met the (50%) retracement objective of the greenback's rally since last September's low (~CAD1.3420). The next retracement (61.8%) is near CAD1.3945, but the greenback is better bid ahead of the North American open. It held CAD1.4055 today and reached nearly CAD1.4170 in European turnover. While the initial hurdle may be near CAD1.4175, given the volatility, a break could see CAD1.4220. Canada's March employment report is a bit dated given the US tariff assault. Many economists fear that the tariff shock and supply-chain disruption could push the Canadian economy into a recession. The risk is on the downside, and any strength will likely be seen as an anomaly. The unemployment rate was at 6.10% in March 2024. Last year's high was in November at 6.9%. It was at 6.6% in January and February and may have ticked up to 6.7%. Canada created 15.5k full-time jobs in the first two months of the year and 49k in the first two months of 2024. Ahead of the swaps, mark is discounting about a 55% chance of a cut at the April 16 meeting, up from about 33% last Friday (March 28) and the previous Friday (March 21).

AUD: In yesterday's dramatic session, the Australian dollar covered the two-cent range that has largely confined the price action for at least the past two months: $0.6200-$0.6400. It posted its highest settlement since March 19, but within Wednesday's range. It has totally unwound yesterday's gains, and more. It has frayed to the $0.6200 area and has not closed below it since mid-January. Australia reported a 0.2% increase in household spending in February and a 3.3% year-over-year rate. The January series was revised to show a 0.5% gain rather than 0.4%. In February 2024, it rose by 4.6% year-over-year. The central bank would likely be more comfortable cutting rates if consumption were weaker. Household spending rose by an average of 0.6% a month in Q4 24, is the strongest quarterly performance since Q3 23. The cumulative 0.7% increase in Jan-Feb this year matches the first two months of 2024. Still, the futures market is confident of a cut at the May 20 RBA meeting. At the end of last week, the market was discounting about a 70% of a quarter-point cut, and now there is about a 20% chance of a 50 bp move. Lastly, note that the White House corrected itself and the reciprocal tariff on New Zealand is 10%, the same as Australia, rather than 20% initially signaled.

MXN: The dollar fell to a new nearly five-month lows yesterday as the greenback was broadly beaten up. It had been turned back from the MXN20.50 area that it probed in the previous three sessions and fell to about MXN19.84, holding the lower Bollinger Band, set two standard-deviations from the 20-day moving average. The greenback is bid and is probing the 20-day moving in Europe (~MXN20.1565). Above there, resistance may be near MXN20.20. Mexico's economic calendar is light ahead of the weekend. Next week's highlight is the March CPI and, barring a significant surprise, the central bank is likely to proceed with another 50 bp cut at the next meeting in the middle of May.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Amazon: Enter Now And Accumulate On Dip
data.description:
Perhaps the most compelling thesis for an Amazon (AMZN) Buy is the recent correction itself. I would definitely consider Amazon in my portfolio with an appetite to accumulate more on further corrections. The last two corrections Amazon saw were from Nov 2021 to Jun 2022 of ~40% and from Aug 2022 to Dec 2022 again ~40%. The current correction is ~ 25% - definitely a rare entry point if one goes by history alone, particularly if the appetite to average on further dip exists.

Financials look stable

We will look into Amazon's financials to get an insight into the fundamentals. Revenue increase in Q1 2025 is pegged at 7% on average as per Analyst Expectations and Amazon’s guidance. This is a potential deceleration from the growth levels of the past four quarters, but still a healthy stat – particularly if we account for possible unfavorable impact due to FX of ~1-2% and a 1% Revenue expansion due to the last year being a leap year (additional day) factored in. In an environment of downturn and lower consumer sentiment, the numbers, if realized, still augur well for a retail exposed stock.

Amazon’s revenue streams diversification and continued growth is actually a super story putting everything together – more so if you see the entry point offered by a 25% correction. Revenue growth has been uniform across segments for the past four quarters. Revenue from the North American segment and the International segment is still greater compared to AWS - these may show signs of deceleration throughout 2025 (which is why the downward revision in growth expectations here). However, it will have low impact on the earnings per share and overall fundamental prospects of investing in AMZN now due to the earnings cushion from AWS.

Operating income and net income path for 2024 has also been robust without those reflecting materially in share prices as it stands today – close to no returns in a year. AWS contribution to income is much higher than its revenue contribution, which looks a good hedge as usual, even in a downturn scenario in North American and International segment earnings, if that remains subdued in 2025. Expectations overall for Q1 2025 is ~$16 billion in operating income, hardly moving from $15.3 billion in YoY comparison for the quarter. Net Income per diluted share is unlikely to move significantly upwards in the upcoming couple of quarters, from the $1.86 number in Q4 2024.

Looking at the dropping growth in earnings (and I am talking of a drop in rate of growth and not absolute earnings here) and a likely stagnation at least in H1 2025, the Cash flow becomes an important metric to see how well-positioned AMZN is to invest into the segments of the Future. Free Cash flow for AMZN has been steady around $36 billion for trailing-twelve-month periods since 2023. This steady and robust Cash flow provides confidence around AMZN’s ability to invest into the high growth and high potential areas, particularly as AMZN does not invest the cash in dividends or repurchases significantly.

The High Growth and High Potential Future

Apart from delivery, sustainability innovations and international market expansions, the significant innovations and progress in AI and AWS is what investors will be keeping a close watch on. Amazon is well into the Amazon Nova Act, advanced AI agents capable of scheduling efficiencies and interacting with web services, which will increase efficiencies in the backend. AI investments are also being made for the front end customer experience – improvements in the Nova series and Alexa and other investments aim to improve the customer conversions, with powerful game-changing abilities ranging from personalized shopping recommendations to even reliable health information.

Investments in Anthropic, in AI, Safety and Research, is another significant step towards integrating AI with AWS. More investments are earmarked for AI infrastructure demand growth in the upcoming quarters. Amazon is well positioned to benefit from the AI wave and benefit further in AWS and the cloud computing market. New revenue streams and leadership position in other areas of AI research and infrastructure are also a huge draw.

A Closer look at AWS – Steam left in new areas as well

From a revenue perspective, AWS has been growing steadily since 2023. However, the AWS mix in the overall revenue pie has not grown significantly in this period compared to other segments. As of Q1 2023, AWS, International and North America segments contributed 17%, 23% and 60% to the revenues. The AWS share of Revenue in Q4 2025, despite the steady AWS revenue growth, remains similar to lower in terms of contribution, pointing to no outrageous growth in the past 2 years. However, it does have a more stable revenue compared to the seasonality of the other segments.

From an earnings perspective, more importantly, Q4 2022 vs Q4 2024 shows all round improvements across all segments, not only AWS. AWS earnings have grown significantly despite only stable growth in revenues, pointing to increasing efficiencies. However, earning growth stories are not headlined by AWS alone. There is more earnings growth in the North America segment in the two-year period and even a bigger turnaround story in earnings for the International segment. The earnings from North America markets have turned a corner and in fact moved into steadily positive to significantly positive territory in the past 2 years. The story is more marked and significant for the International markets, where the turnaround points to potential long-term pointers in a traditionally negative segment for Amazon.

In short, the AWS focused story is not the stand-out growth story yet, to the extent expected. Amazon has actually done exceedingly well in the traditional retail areas in terms of growth, while AWS’s story has been a steady climb, cushioning retail shocks and contributing supporting hand in overall earnings while retail has worked on turnarounds. That is a good story for those who missed the bus and are looking at Amazon as a fresh entry at this point. The full effects of Amazon’s investments in AI and integration with AWS are yet to be seen – definitely the timing is not after a good amount of the journey is already over. I will not be surprised if in the next 2 years, AMZN has new segments in AI reporting earnings. I will also expect the AWS growth story to explode and hope for significant earning moves from here on.

Among other interesting trends emerging, AMZN’s revenues from advertising stood at $56.4 billion in 2024, the highest ever, showing significant promise of growth in the area. Integration of advertisements in streaming, investments in platforms such as Amazon Demand-Size Platform (improvements in advertiser experience), AI integrations and targeting Ad potential using its vast Customer Behavior data – all point to tremendous potential growth from Ads.

Still Compelling Valuations - Buy

While the AMZN journey has a lot of steam left, the critical question is how much of the steam is already factored. That is a difficult question to answer. At the time of going to press, the forward PE for AMZN is around 30. I believe AMZN’s growth orientation – reinvesting operating cash into growth than distribution and strong culture of innovation, still makes it a decent valuation to enter at these levels. First, comparison with peers. Walmart on the retail side, has a P/E of 35. On the Tech side, Alphabet has a P/E of 20 while Microsoft is at 30. Amazon, for its diversified business and Tech and AI focus, should potentially be also compared with freakily high valuations of AI companies. Even those who disagree on AMZN’s valuation (and there aren’t many), will agree that the valuation looks stable in comparison with some other AI bets and not outlandish in a peer comparison. A track record of consistent diversified growth supports the view. Comparison with AMZN’s own historical valuations, shows that the valuation has never been this low in the past 5 years where valuation, the P/E has typically ranged around 40-50 in the past couple of years, barring outlier earnings spikes.

Risk from Tariff Impacts

The thesis risk, as far as Tariff possibilities go in terms of impact on AMZN’s growth, only comes into play if the Tariff details and implementations turn out to be worse than the average expected impact at the moment. I believe as long as the Tariffs are not widespread (details of the sector-specific tariff are still a developing story), most of the impact will be subdued on AMZN’s growth potential on the AWS, AI and Advertisement side.

Risk is more real in the retail segments, particularly in North America. Consumer demand will dampen from the likely impacts of rising costs of goods sold on the marketplace. Huge reliance on China imports overall is likely to face a high tariff hurdle, resulting in higher costs for the customer. Inflation fears will not improve spending power either. Revenue drops of significance are not ruled out. More investments from AMZN to woo customers as marketplace product prices go up will also likely mean a significant impact on earnings. As of Q4 2024, earnings from North America retail were close to 50% of total earnings. A 25% drop in total earnings can be seen if earnings in this segment drops by half. International Retail is less likely to be impacted due to Tariffs. A 20-30% drop in earnings is likely factored in the ~25% correction. Retail has seen cycles of downturn in the past including negative earnings - but usually bounced back in the long term. So, the case for buy the dip and accumulate on further dips still holds.

Buy Call

The supporting points summary - AWS growth is still strong but not really taken off in a big way, in the past couple of years. Valuation is comforting. Investments and potential growth from AWS, AI and Advertising are yet to reflect fully in Financials and now looks like an opportunity to participate in a rally in the long term as those investments start showing up. Particularly as AMZN has corrected decently from the recent top. I would buy AMZN below $185 with an appetite to accumulate further on dips. The upside target is at $260 in a medium to long term of 1-2 years.

data.title: MGK: An Efficient Way To Capitalize On The Future Growth Of AI
data.description:
Overview

Market retractions are a necessary part of the process of building wealth. Market declines presents us with an opportunity to accumulate shares of high quality companies at discounted valuations. I hold over 50 individual positions within my portfolio and I tend to run out of free capital faster than I can add to these positions. Therefore, the most effective way to capitalize on market pullbacks is with an ETF that instantly provides exposure to many of the large-cap names that I want to add to. There are a plethora of different ETFs out there so it can be difficult to decipher which one may be best suited for you. With the projected growth of AI, I believe that Vanguard's Mega Cap Growth Index Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:MGK) is directly positioned to benefit.

MGK has a public inception dating all the way back to 2007, so we have roughly 18 years' worth of performance history to reference. The fund is extremely cost efficient, sporting an expense ratio of 0.07%. MGK puts a greater emphasis on a fewer amount of handpicked companies, which means that it's less diverse than something like the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) or Invesco's QQQ Trust (QQQ). As a result, we can see that MGK has been a bit more volatile in comparison. For instance, MGK's price has fallen by 9% compared to SPY's decrease of 3.9% and QQQ's decrease of 7.1%. However, I believe that long term investors can take advantage of this decline and begin accumulating shares.

If you've followed me for some time, you're probably aware that I typically focus on dividend paying stocks and ETFs. While I do value income generation within my portfolio, I am also very bullish on the growth of the Technology sector over the next decade, mostly due to the growth of the AI sector. Technology companies aren't typically known to prioritize dividends for shareholders, so may dividend funds lack a meaningful Technology allocation. The quickest and most efficient way for me to get the Technology exposure I seek is through an ETF like MGK.

Fund Strategy

According to the fund's most recent overview, MGK has net assets totaling $25B and is spread across 69 different holdings. MGK seeks to track the performance of the CRSP US Mega Cap Growth Index, which represents the companies that make up 70% of the cumulative capitalization of the US Total Stock Market. The fund is passively managed so rebalances and reconstitutions are done on a quarterly basis so that the fund is always up to date with its holdings.

Since MGK tracks the largest growth stocks, it comes as no surprise that the holdings are most heavily allocated towards the Technology sector, making up 52.27% of assets. This is followed by weight in Consumer Cyclical and Communication investments, accounting for 16.25% and 12.49% of assets respectively. However, an investment in MGK is essentially an investment in Technology. Since Technology makes up over half of the fund, the sector has the greatest influence on MGK's daily price movement and total return potential.

Since MGK only maintains exposure to about 69 holdings, the fund is a lot more focused than some peers. For instance, the top ten holdings reveals that Apple (AAPL) is the largest single position at 13.96% of assets. This is followed by an 11.18% weight in Microsoft (MSFT) and a 10.96% weight in NVIDIA (NVDA). The combination of these three holdings makes up over 36% of assets, while the top ten holdings makes up about 63% of assets.

I specifically want this more focused approach because it eliminates the unwanted fluff that a total market ETF may have, such as Vanguard's Total Stock Market Index Fund ETF (VTI) that has over 3,000 holdings within. I believe that the more focused portfolio can lead to outperformance over the long term.

As you are probably aware, many of the top holdings within MGK have seen double-digit price growth over the twelve months. Looking at the chart below, MGK has essentially captured a blended growth rate in comparison. While you may be better off simply choosing to initiate positions in the top performers, this requires a greater capital commitment and there's no guarantee that the top performers will remain the top performers over the next decade. Here are some notable recent events related to some of these top holdings:

  • Tesla (TSLA): Elon Musk will step down from his lead government role, which may help reverse the downward trajectory TSLA has experienced, YTD.
  • NVIDIA (NVDA): Currently in talks to acquire Lepton AI, a company that rents out servers. NVDA has an average Wall Street price target of $171 per share, indicating a potential upside of nearly 55%.
  • Meta Platforms (META): Announced that the company's Llama artificial intelligence models have been downloaded more than 1 billion times, proving its growing competitiveness.

Performance Comparison

As I said at the beginning of this article, it can be very difficult to determine which ETF to choose. While I don't typically care too much if a fund outperforms another by a few percentage points, MGK has outperformed some more notable funds with a wider margin. There are several peers that take a similar approach to focusing on growth stocks, but MGK outperforms the following funds over the last decade. While the underlying weights, expense ratios, and sector allocations may vary, these ETFs all pursue a similar goal. Here are the fund stats laid out next to each other for easy visibility:

Vanguard Mega Cap Growth Index Fund ETF (MGK)

  • Expense ratio: 0.07%.
  • 69 holdings
  • $25B in AUM (assets under management)

Schwab US Large-Cap Growth ETF (SCHG)

  • Expense ratio: 0.04%.
  • 229 holdings
  • $34.8B in AUM

Fidelity Enhanced Large Cap Growth ETF (FELG)

  • Expense ratio: 0.18%
  • 104 holdings
  • $3B in AUM

iShares S&P 500 Growth ETF (IVW)

  • Expense ratio: 0.18%
  • 216 holdings
  • $51.5B in AUM

SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 Growth ETF (SPYG)

  • Expense ratio: 0.04%
  • 212 holdings
  • $30.5B in AUM

The only notable difference to MGK's outperformance may be attributed to its much smaller amount of holdings. MGK has done a great job at assessing large-cap companies and choosing an efficient weight towards each of its top holdings. Assuming positive growth going forward, MGK is the fund that I want to continue accumulating throughout 2025 and beyond.

The trade off of a tighter focus of holdings is that MGK has demonstrated a greater downside potential during market declines. For instance, market indexes declined throughout 2022 as a result of a Fed rate shock. The Fed aggressively hiked interest rates in an attempt to combat the higher than anticipated inflationary data. MGK saw a greater loss than all of these peer ETFs. However, I am personally okay with this tradeoff since I plan to hold MGK for a longer time horizon.

Outlook & Risk

I personally utilize AI tools to some degree in plenty of aspects of my life already. I own a Google Pixel Phone (GOOG), which comes with AI editing software for pictures that I use on a weekly basis. I own a pair of Meta's Ray-Ban (META) smart glasses that have AI capabilities which allows me to send messages without even pulling out my phone. I play video games listed on Microsoft's (MSFT) Game Pass service, that uses AI to build procedurally generated worlds. These are just some of the real-world examples that are already relevant in today's world. While these implementations may seem small, there is a future where the utility of AI is so much more advanced.

Imagine self-driving robo-taxis from Tesla (TSLA) or Uber (UBER) that can get implemented on a global scale. We may see a world where AI-driven robotics assist doctors with complicated medical procures. AI can improve infrastructure related to urban planning and sustainability efforts, which will improve the quality of our lives. Then there's the whole AI aspect of blockchain technologies in the crypto world.

MGK makes it easy to align yourself to benefit from these innovations since many of the top holdings are investing in products or services that leverage AI technology. Additionally, there are several research papers that suggest there is still massive growth ahead for the sector. For instance, Markets & Markets estimates that the size of the AI sector can increase at a CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 35.7% through 2030.

However, there have been contradictory reports that believe the average annual growth of markets can slow in the future, driven by slower sales growth of these technology companies. Goldman Sachs released a report that estimated the S&P 500 will average an annual return of 3% for the next decade, which is a far way off from the +10% average the market has grown accustomed to.

The idea here is that it's simply unsustainable for these companies to continue reporting double-digit sales growth year after year. For instance, only 11% of S&P 500 companies have been able to maintain a double-digit sales growth for more than 10 consecutive years. While I think this outlook is overly-pessimistic, it's certainly a possibility.

Perhaps we see a market environment that begins to question the growth of AI markets. There's a risk that it will take longer than anticipated for growth to materialize throughout the technology sector, accompanied by growing competition overseas. For instance, we saw the negative market reaction as a result of DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) earlier in the year. There's a chance that investor sentiment around the sector changes, which can cause capital to flow out of tech and into other areas of the market.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I rate MGK as a buy as the fund is an efficient way to instant gain exposure to the highest quality large-cap companies in the world. If you have a bullish long-term perspective on the growth of AI and technology, MGK can be a way to capitalize on the future growth. The markets recently pull back caused by ongoing tension related to Trump's tariff proposals has presented an attractive opportunity for accumulation. MGK has a fewer amount of holdings compared to peer ETFs, but I believe this can ultimately lead to outperformance as it eliminates the extra fluff. The fund is also very affordable, with a low expense ratio of 0.07%.

data.title: SOXX ETF: An Attractive Entry Point To The Mainstream Adoption Of AI
data.description:
The performance of iShares Semiconductor ETF (NASDAQ:SOXX) remains far from encouraging due to the bumpy ride of the main semi players since the beginning of the year. My previous piece on SOXX pointed out the 5G networks and GPU demand for data centers as the main pillars for the upside potential. I believe that SOXX at current levels is an attractive Buy opportunity ahead of the semiconductor market growth. My estimate stipulates up to 35% upside potential, which could come from the fund's reasonable exposure to top-tier semiconductor manufacturers in light of the recent market conditions, emphasis on analog chip providers, and an attractive valuation.

Fund overview

SOXX ETF is established to track the performance of the ICE Semiconductor Index, which consists of stocks of the leading semiconductor manufacturers and equipment providers. The fund remained quite concentrated around its top 5 holdings, which account for 37.5% of the fund's portfolio of 30 companies. However, there is a shift in the core positions with Texas Instruments (TXN) occupying 8.1% of the portfolio, followed by Nvidia Corp. (NVDA) with 7.7% share; Broadcom (AVGO) with 7.4%; Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) with 7.3%; and Qualcomm (QCOM) with 6.6% share, where Intel (INTC) was dropped from the top 5 holdings. In the meantime, the net asset value of the fund grew by 24.1% since my previous article up to $10.8 billion, which costs the investors 0.35% on annum.

Looking at the performance since the beginning of the year, the semiconductor stocks and the technology sector as a whole have been on a bumpy ride. SOXX has tumbled 13% to date and is now about 29% down from its record high. The funds direct competitors, VanEck Semiconductor ETF (SMH) and First Trust Nasdaq Semiconductor ETF (FTXL) share the same direction, while SPDR S&P Semiconductor ETF (XSD) and Invesco Semiconductor ETF (PSE) fell further.

The reasoning behind the lagging performance of the semiconductor and the technology sector as a whole this year could be linked to trade disputes about tariffs and the worries about future investment levels in AI following the outbreak of DeepSeek’s models. However, the mainstream adoption of GenAI is yet to take place, where the hardware infrastructure spending is anticipated at least to double in the near term in order to meet the AI workloads. Hence, I believe that the current downfall of semi-socks represents an attractive buying opportunity going forward.

Industry dynamics

Considering the industry condition, global chip sales marked an 18% YoY increase in January, maintaining momentum following the strong semiconductor market in 2024. The recent data confirmed the continued steady annual growth rates of semiconductor sales, but WSTS expects the semiconductor market to slow down somewhat during the year.

Still, according to forecasts, chip sales should increase by 11.2% YoY in 2025 following 19% growth last year. If we try to make a linear interpolation, the forecast implies a 1.3% monthly sequential growth during the year (dotted blue line). We can also notice that the blue line is positioned higher than the green and red lines, indicating that the market could hit new highest-ever sales numbers for each month. The growth pattern provides quite encouraging prospects for the semiconductor players, where the memory and logic segments should rebound the most.

Industry risk factors

The semi-industry is affected by the technological standoff between the US and China, which continues to escalate. The tariff hike on imports from China is expected to put pressure on the PC and smartphone markets, creating a risk of a decreased demand for semiconductor chips. In addition, the release of DeepSeek’s AI models triggered significant volatility in the market and uncertainty about the future scale of investments. The spread of such cost-efficient models could lead to a slowdown of investments in large data centers and reduce the demand for advanced chips.

Investment inference

I will keep to the weighted average target prices estimation approach based on securities that make up the fund’s portfolio in order to assess the SOXX potential. Taking the consensus estimates, the model infers a significant upside potential for the SOXX ETF of around 35%, which implies a target price of $256.

We can notice that the majority of the holding has significant gains anticipated by analysts, which could bring SOXX near to its all-time high level. In particular, the largest contribution to the estimated upside could come from Nvidia (4.22%), Broadcom (3.31%), AMD (3.09%), Marvell (2.35%), and Qualcomm (1.96%). It’s obvious that the main upside should be delivered by semiconductor manufacturers, since they are the prime drivers of artificial intelligence development. In addition, the fund has more than 25% of its portfolio invested in analog chip providers, which are expected to face a strong increase in demand this year.

Overall, such a strong forecast for semi-players supports the opinion that the AI boom hasn’t come yet. The rapid development of the technology requires a lot of computing power to run the advanced models. What’s more, the emergence of more cost-efficient models could accelerate the diffusion of AI in various sectors of the economy, thus further stimulating the demand for computing power for inference operations.

Conclusion

In my opinion, SOXX provides an incredible opportunity to buy at a discount the expected AI hardware spending growth. Compared to SMH, the fund has far less exposure to NVDA and Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM), which could facilitate investors to avoid the current uncertainty regarding the AI investments and the escalation of the China-Taiwan tensions.

The valuation of SOXX exhibits a downward trend since the beginning of the year, where the forward P/E decreased by 10.4% to 20x. Going forward, the latter ratio is still 10% below the 3-year median of 22.2x and is lower than the broad technology select sector’s 23.4x valuation represented by XLK. As a result, I will keep my Buy rating on SOXX as the ETF has an attractive valuation, providing a good entry point to capture the anticipated growth of the semiconductor market in 2025.

data.title: CMNIX: An Opportunity For Ballast In Turbulent Markets
data.description:
By Eli Pars, CFA

During the first quarter, markets once again took investors on a roller coaster ride. The market’s anticipation of business- and market-friendly policies (e.g., lower taxes and less regulation) gave way to anxiety about the impact of tariffs on inflation, consumer activity, and economic growth. The S&P 500 fought back from a 10% peak-to-trough decline, but still finished down more than 4% on the quarter, with a significant rotation away from tech stocks. After a disappointing 4Q loss and a lackluster 2024, the bond market made its way back into investors' good graces, benefiting from the market’s flight to areas of the market with perceived safety.

Through it all, Calamos Market Neutral Income Fund (CMNIX) continued to deliver on our aim of providing steady gains, regardless of the market’s direction. Our approach — combining an arbitrage sleeve and hedged equity sleeve — has little exposure to interest rate opportunity or risk. During the quarter, this resulted in the fund’s advance trailing traditional bonds slightly, but over time, our approach has delivered a better — and more consistent — historical return experience than traditional fixed income.

The Case for CMNIX

Outperforming Bonds over the Long Term

The Appeal of the “Middle Road”: CMNIX Versus Stocks and Bonds

Growth of $10,000, six months to 3/31/25

How We Are Positioned

Our strategy gives us the flexibility to adjust our allocation on an ongoing basis. Especially in fast-moving markets, we believe this gives Calamos Market Neutral Income Fund an edge over funds with more static rebalancing strategies. Throughout the quarter, we took advantage of volatility and were active in booking profits and rebalancing our hedges. Our hedged equity allocation crept up at the margin. Both our convertible arbitrage and hedged equity strategy contributed positively to performance, with the former leading.

CMNIX Allocation

Hedged Equity. This sleeve is positioned to provide upside participation in the equity market, while seeking to significantly limit downside exposure. The volatility of the first quarter gave us opportunities to lock in an attractive spread between the calls we were writing and the put spreads we created; overall, our hedge remains toward the more defensive end of its long-term historical range, although slightly less defensive than at points in recent quarters.

Arbitrage. Our arbitrage sleeve is built around a core allocation to convertible arbitrage, with opportunistic allocations to other strategies, such as merger arbitrage and SPAC arbitrage. Although the fund’s allocation to convertible arbitrage decreased marginally during the period, our outlook remains constructive.

The first quarter saw brisk new issuance, with companies bringing nearly $23 billion in new issuance to market globally, with structures that were attractive on the whole. Looking forward, although issuance could be tempered if equity markets stay volatile, the interest-rate environment continues to incentivize issuers to choose convertibles over nonconvertible debt to contain borrowing costs.

Global Convertible Issuance: Healthy Year-to-Date Levels ($bil)

Our allocations to merger arbitrage and special purpose acquisition company (SPAC) arbitrage held steady over the quarter. Both are highly idiosyncratic strategies. We expect to see the Trump administration turn more attention to lowering regulatory burdens in the coming months, which could boost the opportunity set in merger arbitrage.

Outlook

The magnitude and reach of President Trump’s April 2 tariff announcement took markets by surprise, and we’re seeing a violent reaction in the immediate wake. However, since Trump 2.0 began, we’ve seen that nothing is set in stone when it comes to tariffs. And while it’s hard today to look past tariffs and the potential “worse-case” impacts on the markets and economy, other areas of Trump’s policy agenda could provide some countervailing tailwinds.

No doubt, there will be a lot of twists and turns, which we believe play to our strengths and the potential opportunity of Calamos Market Neutral Income Fund. One of our bedrock principles is that we can’t predict what will happen in the markets, so we need to position the fund for as many outcomes as possible. To us, this seems like an especially good approach in the current environment.
